question: Which statement about migrating active elements that live inside subprocesses is correct?

options.text: Mapping instructions must be provided for both the inner active element and the enclosing subprocess; embedded subprocesses cannot be migrated to event subprocesses.
Only the inner active element requires a mapping; the enclosing subprocess is inferred automatically from element IDs.
An active embedded subprocess can be migrated to an event subprocess as long as the inner active element is also mapped.
Migration may change the scope of a subprocess, for example by moving an inner element out to the top-level process scope.

a.text: To migrate an active element inside a subprocess, a mapping instruction must be provided from the source subprocess ID to the target subprocess ID, in addition to mapping the inner element. Migrating an embedded subprocess to an event subprocess is explicitly not allowed.

b.text: Mapping the inner element is not enough. The enclosing subprocess must also be mapped with its own instruction; nothing is inferred automatically by ID.

c.text: You cannot migrate an active embedded subprocess to an event subprocess, regardless of whether the inner element is also mapped.

d.text: Changing the scope of a subprocess during migration is not possible. The element must stay inside an equivalent enclosing scope.

explanation: Active elements located inside subprocesses can be migrated, but both the inner element and the enclosing subprocess require explicit mapping instructions. In addition, an embedded subprocess cannot be migrated to an event subprocess, and the scope of a subprocess cannot be changed by migration.
